Yes — now that you point at it, it's all over the transcript. Two concrete symptoms:

**1. Agents replying to *themselves*.** The p-tag on these events points back at the sender:
- `<codex-indigo-lima-590> @codex-indigo-lima-590 Acknowledged. #375 is the source of truth…`
- `<ember-spark-343-codex> @ember-spark-343-codex: I'm not the #363 owner…`

An agent got a message, resolved the recipient to *itself*, and answered itself.

**2. The @mention text doesn't match the actual member handles** — the codename order is flipped:
- Agents write `@codex-gale-yonder-843`, but the roster handle is `@gale-yonder-843-codex`.
- `@codex-clay-lima-461`, `@codex-indigo-lima-590`, `@ember-spark-343-codex` — none of these appear in the current `<members>` list at all; the live members are `-codex`-*suffixed* (`hazel-tango-569-codex`, `zephyr-vale-191-codex`, etc.).

So the **visible @mention and the p-tag routing have diverged**. When an agent types `@codex-gale-yonder-843`, the codename resolver either fails to map it to the right pubkey or maps it to the wrong one — so the intended recipient is never actually p-tagged, and the message bounces to self / to the management identity instead. That's the same substrate feeding the #375 classifier loop: kind:9 events landing on the wrong p-tag and cycling.

The interesting part is that the tail of the thread — the long ivy ↔ lark #377 review — used the *correct* suffixed handles (`@ivy-grove-125-codex`, `@lark-drift-295-codex`) and routed fine. The breakage clusters on the `codex-<name>`-*prefixed* form. That smells like two different codename-emission paths: one produces `role-name` (resolves), one produces `codex-name` (doesn't).

Want me to trace the codename → p-tag resolution path and pin down where the prefixed form gets emitted vs. where it's resolved? That'd tell us whether this is the same root cause as #375 or a separate handle-format bug worth its own issue.

Done — deleted `~/.tenex/edge` (freed 3.6 GB). The live daemon home `~/.tenex-edge` is untouched. (The rest of `~/.tenex/` — `agents`, `cli`, `config.json`, etc. — I left alone; you only flagged `edge`.)

## What's going on with slate-falcon

slate-falcon isn't broken and isn't dead — **the daemon is.** Its event-processing loop is deadlocked, so no message you send reaches any agent.

**The evidence:**

| Signal | Reading |
|---|---|
| Backing process (pid 79041) | **alive** — `codex --yolo resume`, sleeping normally |
| Daemon process (pid 32195) | alive but **0.0% CPU** → blocked on a lock, *not* spinning |
| Last kind:9 (chat) event ingested | **05:26:36** — nothing in ~3 hours |
| `relay.log` (raw relay frames) | still writing at 08:25 → WebSocket is fine, receiving |
| `offline-agent-mention handler` dispatches | **8,992** between 11:30 yesterday and 05:26 today |

**The mechanism** (this is issue #375 manifesting live): the daemon's management/offline-mention classifier treats ordinary kind:9 prose that p-tags an agent as a dispatchable command. Two pubkeys (`09417c93`, `af34d6c4`) got hammered ~3,300 times each in an ~9,000-iteration loop. Somewhere in that path — almost certainly the reentrant-lock class you've hit before (metadata lock held across a broadcast) — the ingestion task **wedged at 05:26:36** and never recovered. The RPC socket runs on a separate task, so `who`/`list-sessions` still answer and still show everyone as "just now" (status heartbeats arrive on a path that doesn't touch the wedged lock) — which is exactly why the agents *look* online while ignoring you.

So "slate-falcon keeps ignoring me" = the daemon stopped delivering your messages as turns ~3 hours ago. Every agent on this machine is equally deaf right now; slate-falcon is just the one you happened to be talking to.

**The fix** is a daemon restart — it releases the deadlock, and the running codex/claude sessions get revived and resume delivery. That's disruptive though: it bounces the coordination substrate for all ~40 agents mid-work. 

Want me to restart the daemon now? And separately — this is the live reproduction of #375; I can attach these concrete numbers (8,992 dispatches, wedge at 05:26:36, the two hot pubkeys, 0%-CPU deadlock signature) to the issue so the fix targets the actual deadlock, not just the classifier breadth.

**No — #375 is still OPEN.** Title: *"Management classifier creates kind:9 reply feedback loops."* No fix has landed:
- Issue state: `OPEN`, `closedAt: null`.
- No commit anywhere references 375 (`git log --all --grep=375` is empty).
- The one PR that matched ("#192, membership docs") is a false positive — merged Jul 2, before #375 was even filed on Jul 11.

So what you're seeing on this machine right now **is #375 unfixed, biting in production**: the ~9,000-iteration offline-mention loop is exactly the feedback loop the issue describes, and it's what wedged the daemon at 05:26. The issue was filed with the diagnosis (classifier boundary fault) but nobody has implemented the fix — and notably it only captured the *loop*, not the *deadlock-and-wedge* consequence I just found.

Two concrete next steps I'd suggest:
1. **Restart the daemon** to unstick delivery right now (slate-falcon and everyone else start responding again).
2. **Add my findings to #375** — the deadlock signature (0% CPU, ingestion stalled at 05:26:36 while the WS keeps receiving), the 8,992-dispatch count, and the two hot pubkeys — so whoever picks it up fixes the actual wedge, not just the classifier breadth.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/pablofernandez/.claude/skills/tts # TTS Skill Generate spoken audio from text with a Kokoro-compatible endpoint. ## Input text rules Use natural sentences for best results. - **Acronyms**: Write as you'd type them — `CLI`, `API`, `HTTP`, etc. The skill auto-converts to `C L I`, `A P I`, `H T T P` for letter-by-letter pronunciation. - **Abbreviations**: Expand when pronunciation matters — `AI` → `artificial intelligence`, `DB` → `database`, `API` → `application programming interface`. - **Code & paths**: Avoid or rephrase — instead of `run /usr/local/bin/script`, say `run the script`. - **Punctuation**: Natural pauses work; avoid excessive symbols. ## Available voices American English deterministic pool: `af_bella`, `af_heart`, `af_kore`, `af_nova`, `af_sarah`, `am_michael`, `am_puck` Other languages & accents available: British English, Japanese, Mandarin, French, Italian, Portuguese. ## Agent identity Always pass `--introduction` with a brief spoken preamble of 30 words or fewer: ```text Agent <name> here, working on <short user-meaningful task>. ``` Use a phrase the listener can understand without opening a tracker, such as `improving TTS identity cues` or `refactoring the lib module`, not bare tracker labels or numeric references. The script decides whether to prepend it. If the same agent spoke within ten minutes, the intro is skipped; if another agent spoke since then, or enough time passed, the intro is included again. Pass `--agent-name` only when the session has a specific identifier beyond the harness name. Use names like `quinn-delta-306`; do not use generic values like `codex` or `claude`. When using `--agent-name`, omit voice selection so the script can choose a deterministic voice from that name. Use `--voice-id` only when a specific voice is required. ## Subject Use `--subject` when a substantive update benefits from a scannable topic in the queue. Write 5 to 10 words that name the outcome or subject clearly. The subject is displayed prominently in the player and spoken immediately after any introduction, before the message body. Skip `--subject` when it would add ceremony without useful context, such as a brief conversational response, acknowledgement, or follow-up question. ## Playback behavior By default, `scripts/tts` generates the MP3 in the foreground, then queues playback in the background so the agent sees endpoint/setup failures before the command returns. On macOS, the first audible request starts a resident menu-bar app. It owns the playback queue, shows queued/current/recent speech, and provides pause, resume, 15-second skip controls, and replay by clicking a recent row. The current item uses a podcast-style player with an approximate word-progress transcript. While an item is playing or paused, the popup focuses on its full player and transcript; the queue and recent history return when playback becomes idle. Queue rows include the text, voice, agent name, and any harness, full session identifier, subject, and workspace metadata available in the calling environment. Use `scripts/tts-menu status` to check whether TTS is playing and inspect queue counts. Use `scripts/tts-menu status --json` for structured status. Use `scripts/tts-menu start`, `stop`, or `restart` to manage the menu-bar process. If the native app is disabled or cannot start, background playback workers use the speech gate so only one audible TTS job speaks at a time. Set `TTS_MACOS_MENU=0` to force this fallback. On macOS, the script checks scriptable media apps before generation and the active playback backend checks again before playback. If Music or Spotify is already playing, it pauses them and resumes them a few seconds after playback ends. - Use `--no-play` to generate the MP3 without playback and print its path only after the file exists. - Use `--voice-id voice` to choose an explicit voice. - Use `--no-media-pause` or `TTS_MEDIA_CONTROL=0` to skip media pausing. - Use `--resume-delay seconds` or `TTS_RESUME_DELAY_SECONDS=seconds` to change the resume delay. - Use `TTS_MEDIA_APPS="Music,Spotify"` to customize the checked apps. Queue records, process state, and logs live under the TTS state directory, normally `~/.local/state/tts/`. `./scripts/tts --agent-name quinn-delta-306 --introduction "Agent Quinn here, working on TTS." --subject "Playback queue ownership is now explicit" "The fix is ready."` will speak the introduction when needed, then the subject, then the body. `./scripts/tts --no-play --voice-id af_bella "Hello world"` will generate the MP3, print its output path, skip playback, and use an explicit voice. Spoke it. Summary of what I said:

**How obvious is the fix?** The *loop* is obvious in the code — `demux.rs:170` unconditionally calls `offline_mention::dispatch` for every mentioned chat, guarded only by an "already alive" check. The narrow fix is clear (don't auto-spawn on agent-authored messages + rate-limit per target), but *what should legitimately spawn* is a design call, not a one-liner.

**How sure are we this is the problem?** The loop is proven — 8,992 dispatches logged. That closes it. Here's the corrected, evidence-based diagnosis — and I need to retract two things I said earlier.

## What I got wrong
1. **"Stop agents from triggering each other" was the wrong fix.** You're right — agent-to-agent is the whole point. Ignore that.
2. **The daemon is not deadlocked.** It's updating `last_seen` to 08:46 right now. My "wedged at 05:26" claim was wrong — there simply were no new chat events after 05:26 (quiet early morning), and status heartbeats kept flowing the whole time.

## Why slate-falcon actually isn't replying

It's not ignoring anyone — **the messages physically never reached it.** Concretely:

- **3 messages are stuck `pending` in the inbox, `delivered_at=0`** — including yours ("what do you mean 'runtime host-relay switch', that makes no sense", the UX/arrow-button one, and a handover request). Everything **up to 22:50 Jul 11 was delivered; everything after is stuck.**
- The break lines up exactly with when codex **finished its last turn** — its rollout log ends with `task_complete` at **20:08 UTC Jul 11**, and it's been **parked idle at the codex prompt** since (`working=0`).
- **The session has no endpoint the daemon can inject into.** Its codex process (79041) is running in a **plain iTerm tab** (`codex ← node ← zsh ← login ← iTerm`), *not* a daemon-managed pane — and the **tmux server is dead** ("no server running"). While codex is actively turning, its own hook drains the inbox, so delivery works. The moment it goes idle, delivery depends on the daemon **injecting a doorbell into its PTY** — and there's nowhere to inject. So messages pile up forever.

**That also explains the "offline mention for an online agent" smell you flagged:** the daemon dispatched the offline-mention handler for slate-falcon's *own* pubkey (`7345f8d7`) **236 times** while `alive=1`. It's the fallback firing because the daemon keeps seeing mentions of an agent it *can't deliver to* — the `has_alive` guard then no-ops it, so it just logs and spins. The real defect isn't "offline dispatch for an online agent"; it's that **an idle, alive session with no drivable endpoint is a silent delivery black hole.**

(Side note worth knowing: this codex is at **104M input tokens** — it's an ancient, near-maxed context. Even once woken it may be near its limit.)

## Remedies

**Immediate (get your messages to it):** go to that iTerm tab and run `tenex-edge inbox` (or just press Enter / type a nudge) — codex will drain the 3 pending messages itself. That's the only thing that reaches it right now.

**Systemic (the real bug):** sessions launched **outside the daemon's tmux** (manually resumed in iTerm) register and heartbeat as `alive`, but the daemon has no PTY/pane to doorbell — so idle delivery silently fails while the agent looks online. The fix is on the delivery side, not the "agents talking" side: the daemon must either (a) refuse to mark a session deliverable when it has no injectable endpoint (so mentions surface as genuinely-undeliverable instead of vanishing), or (b) support injecting into non-tmux/iTerm PTYs by tty, or (c) reap/adopt orphaned sessions when the tmux server dies.

Want me to (1) nudge the iTerm session so your 3 messages land, and/or (2) file this as a proper issue — "idle session with no injectable endpoint silently black-holes inbox delivery" — separate from #375?

Base directory for this skill: /Users/pablofernandez/.claude/skills/tenex-edge-dev # tenex-edge development live lab Use this skill when validating tenex-edge changes in a real local environment: a host croissant relay, isolated container backends, real host Claude/Codex/OpenCode auth, PTY and ACP/app-server agents, relay-level traffic, hook logs, and Nostr events inspected with `nak`. This is the replacement mindset for the old scripted e2e harness. Prefer a small live lab that can be inspected over a mocked shortcut. The objective is not to prove model quality; it is to prove that the real agents, hooks, relay traffic, launch paths, and injected context work together. ## Resource Map Read these files from this skill directory as needed: - `references/live-lab-workflow.md`: the complete start-to-finish lab procedure, including single-agent, multi-agent, direct harness, and `tenex-edge launch` flows. - `references/container-backends.md`: how host credentials, container state, profile configs, model choices, and launch modes fit together. - `references/acp-backends.md`: the two-file bundle contract, structured profiles, smoke/headless launch workflow, and ACP-specific troubleshooting. - `references/observability.md`: how to inspect PTY sessions, croissant logs, Nostr events, hook logs, daemon logs, and what counts as evidence. - `references/troubleshooting.md`: common failures and the concrete checks to run before escalating. - `scripts/start-croissant-relay`: starts croissant as a host process, creates a per-run work directory, waits for NIP-11, and writes `lab.env`. - `scripts/write-container-profiles`: writes disposable backend config plus the current `harnesses.json` and `agents/<slug>.json` launch contract. - `scripts/launch-agent`: runs a raw provider CLI, an ACP/app-server smoke, or `tenex-edge launch`; structured launches are headless and PTY launches attach. - `scripts/probe-lab`: captures relay NIP-11, relay logs, and selected Nostr event kinds into a probe directory. - `scripts/cleanup-lab`: stops recorded agent containers and then stops the relay process. ## Non-Negotiables - Use real host AI auth. The container runner defaults to `TENEX_EDGE_CONTAINER_HOST_AUTH=1`; it mounts host auth directories read-only and projects credential files into isolated container state. - Do not create fake provider files or fake logins unless the user explicitly asks for a non-agent smoke test. - Do not print secrets. Never show auth files, provider files, generated `nsec` values, or raw private keys in the transcript or final report. - Keep fabric state isolated. Generated state belongs under `.container-state/` or a temp live-lab work directory, not in the host `~/.tenex-edge`. - Run croissant on the host at `/tmp/croissant-smallmap` when present, else `${HOME}/Work/croissant`; set `TENEX_EDGE_DEV_CROISSANT_DIR` to override. Containers point at the host relay URL; croissant itself does not need a container. - Use the cheapest useful model for each provider. The lab only needs enough model ability to run commands and surface UI/hook behavior. - Use `tenex-edge launch` when validating reattach, injection, or hosted lifecycle behavior. Direct runs are foreground auth/plugin checks; `__acp-smoke` proves structured transport handshake, prompt, and resume. - Harness configuration is two-file state under `TENEX_EDGE_HOME`: `harnesses.json` defines bundles and `agents/<slug>.json` selects a bundle via `harness`. The filename is plural; there is no `harness.json` surface. - Use `tenex-edge who` for identity/fabric inspection. Do not rely on obsolete identity commands. ## Standard Start From the tenex-edge repo root: ```bash git status -sb bash containers/tenex-edge/run build-image bash containers/tenex-edge/run doctor ``` `doctor` must verify every installed backend/transport tool, `nak`, and the selected profile's provider auth plus hook/plugin installation. If auth checks fail, stop and report the missing host path; do not silently switch to new credentials. Start a relay: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/start-croissant-relay ``` The command prints an `env=.../lab.env` path. Use that exact file for the rest of the run: ```bash LAB_ENV=/tmp/tenex-edge-live-lab-YYYYmmdd-HHMMSS/lab.env skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/write-container-profiles "${LAB_ENV}" claude-acp ``` Prewarm and verify the exact profile before launching the agent UI: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude-acp doctor skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" smoke claude-acp ``` For multiple backends: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/write-container-profiles "${LAB_ENV}" \ claude-acp codex-app-server opencode-acp ``` The writer generates disposable local Nostr keys, writes `.container-state/<profile>/tenex/edge/harnesses.json` plus the selecting agent file, and whitelists every generated pubkey in every profile. Model-provider auth still comes from the host credential mounts. ## Launch Patterns Direct harness run in the foreground: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" direct claude --model haiku ``` Run through `tenex-edge launch` in portable PTY mode: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude tenex-edge channel init skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" launch claude --model haiku ``` Codex example: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" direct codex -m gpt-5.3-codex-spark ``` OpenCode example: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" direct opencode-ollama "${TENEX_EDGE_OPENCODE_OLLAMA_MODEL:-ollama/deepseek-r1:8b}" ``` If a CLI rejects the model flag, record the rejection and fall back to the cheapest configured model that works. Do not block the lab on an exact model name unless the feature under test depends on that provider/model. Structured launch examples: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude-acp tenex-edge channel init TENEX_EDGE_DEV_PROMPT="Run tenex-edge who and summarize the self header." \ skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/launch-agent "${LAB_ENV}" launch claude-acp ``` Use `codex-app-server` and `opencode-acp` the same way. Configure their model through the profile-writer environment described in `container-backends.md`; do not pass provider CLI flags to an ACP/app-server launch. ## Inspecting The Run Inspect PTY launch sessions: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude tenex-edge pty list bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude tenex-edge pty attach <pty-id> ``` Drive a prompt or hook-like injection: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude tenex-edge pty inject <pty-id> \ "Run tenex-edge who and summarize the self header." ``` Probe everything into files: ```bash skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/probe-lab "${LAB_ENV}" <agent-session> ``` Also inspect: ```bash bash containers/tenex-edge/run --profile claude tenex-edge debug hook-tail tail -n 200 .container-state/claude/tenex/edge/daemon.log tail -n 200 .container-state/claude/tenex/edge/relay.log ``` Croissant logs all inbound/outbound traffic and rejected event reasons to the relay log named in `lab.env`. Use those logs together with `nak` event probes and transport-specific evidence. ACP/app-server launches print `[tenex-edge acp] session: ...` and have no PTY to attach; prove them with the smoke output, launch session id, daemon/delivery logs, and relay events. ## Evidence Standard A useful report contains: - relay URL, run id, profile names, and whether this was direct or launch mode - exact agent commands and accepted model flags - PTY attach output for PTY agents, or `__acp-smoke` plus headless session and delivery evidence for ACP/app-server agents - croissant log excerpts showing traffic or rejection reasons - `nak` evidence for the expected event kinds - hook-tail or daemon log evidence when testing hook injection - pass/fail tied to the feature under test, plus the next failing command if it did not pass ## Simple Agent Validation Prompt Give this to a simple agent to validate that the skill works: ```text Use the tenex-edge-dev skill. Start a fresh local croissant relay on the host without forcing port 9888. Generate a `claude-acp` container profile with real host Claude auth, disposable fabric keys, `harnesses.json`, and a selecting agent file. Run profile doctor and the ACP smoke, initialize the workspace channel, then launch the headless Claude ACP agent with an initial prompt asking it to run `tenex-edge who`. Collect the ACP session id, daemon/delivery and croissant logs, hook evidence, and nak relay probes. Clean up with scripts/cleanup-lab. Do not print secret or auth file contents. Report exact commands/results; if it fails, write concise lessons to the skill. ``` ARGUMENTS: smoke-test the new `tenex-edge my session pty-wrap-me --self` command: launch an agent OUTSIDE a daemon PTY (reproduce the non-PTY / no pty_session alias condition), send it a mention while idle to confirm it black-holes, then run pty-wrap-me and verify it re-homes into a daemon PTY and the pending mention now delivers

**PASS — all 5 steps, end-to-end against a real Claude agent.** `pty-wrap-me` genuinely works: it re-homes a live non-PTY session into a daemon PTY and unblocks its stuck mail. The earlier "not exercised" caveat is now cleared.

The proof chain:

| Step | Result |
|---|---|
| **1. Non-PTY repro** | Direct `claude --model haiku` under a raw `pty.fork` — session `te-18c1ca2f…`, no daemon PTY |
| **2. Confirmed non-PTY** | `pty list` empty; aliases have `harness_session` but **no `pty_session`**. *Bonus:* the #405 warning appeared verbatim in its turn context |
| **3. Idle black-hole** | Mention from another pubkey → inbox `pending, delivered_at=0`, no inject (`DeferNoEndpoint`) |
| **4. The fix** | `my session pty-wrap-me --self` → `re-homed into daemon PTY claude-…-24` (exit 0). Refusals verified: outside-session → exit 1; second call → `already inside a live daemon PTY` no-op |
| **5. Re-home verified** | old PID killed; **same session, same pubkey** now in `pty list` with a live `pty_session` alias; the stuck mention **flipped to `injected, delivered_at>0`** and the daemon logged `pending messages delivered to endpoint` |

It ran the actual merged binary (`45e18b63` confirmed in the build), real host auth, isolated state, cleaned up after itself, and left your host fleet daemon untouched.

**One thing to note (environment, not a product bug):** the first relay came up on the **default shared port 19888** and collided with another agent from your running fleet that had already created the `workspace` group — so the lab's keys got `blocked: unknown member`. Re-running on a unique port fixed it. That's the shared-port hazard the skill warns about, and it's a real trap worth keeping in mind whenever you run labs while the fleet is live.

So the full arc is closed: **non-PTY idle black-hole diagnosed → `session kill --self` (#404), preamble warning (#405), `pty-wrap-me` (#407) shipped → live-verified.** The only deliberately-unaddressed piece is the *systemic* fix (making `DeferNoEndpoint` itself stop silently no-op'ing — emit a failure event or auto-rehome), which we scoped out earlier. Want me to file that as a follow-up issue so it's not lost, or leave it?
wouldn't it be better if the e2e harness just uses a random port for the local relay?

Works as intended:

- **Random by default:** three consecutive runs picked `44864`, `47590`, `33477` — spread across the 20000–50000 range, each verified free before returning.
- **Overrides preserved:** `TENEX_EDGE_DEV_RELAY_PORT_BASE=30500` → `30500`, and `TENEX_EDGE_DEV_RELAY_PORT` still forces an exact port (unchanged downstream).
- Full script passes `bash -n`.

The change (in `~/.claude/skills/tenex-edge-dev/scripts/start-croissant-relay`):
- Default base is now `20000 + RANDOM % 30000` per run instead of a fixed `19888`.
- It retries up to 5 fresh random bases, so the rare TOCTOU race (two labs binding the same "free" port at once) just re-rolls instead of colliding — while a *pinned* base fails fast if fully occupied rather than looping pointlessly.

So concurrent labs and the live fleet now practically never share a relay, which kills the NIP-29 group-ownership collision at its root. That was skill tooling, not the repo, so nothing to PR.

Net state of this whole thread: the non-PTY delivery gap is diagnosed, fixed (#404/#405/#407), live-verified, and the lab hazard that showed up during verification is closed too. The only thing still parked is the optional systemic fix (making `DeferNoEndpoint` self-heal instead of silently no-op) — say the word if you want it filed.
